cataloging_subject.label: The Canterbury Tales (Dd.4.24/Egerton 2726 Furnivall representation)
cataloging_subject.scope_note: Geoffrey Chaucer's unfinished Middle English pilgrimage frame and conventional twenty-four-tale collection as represented in Frederick J. Furnivall's two-part 1901-1902 Dd.4.24 edition, completed across Dd's lacunae and ending from Egerton MS 2726. The boundary includes prologues, links, Retraction, Skeat-derived omitted-line appendix, bilingual Angelus ad Virginem from Arundel MS 248, and exactly thirty issued Hooper woodcut surfaces (twenty-three Ellesmere-derived and seven Gg.4.27-derived). It excludes broad society matter and keeps abstract work, witness readings, printed edition, images, digital encoding, and repository artifact distinct.

catalog_summary.title: The Canterbury Tales
catalog_summary.author: Geoffrey Chaucer
catalog_summary.date: chiefly late 1380s-1400; this representation issued 1901-1902 with a foreword dated 1903

titles.value: The Cambridge MS. Dd. 4. 24. of Chaucer's Canterbury Tales, Completed by the Egerton MS. 2726 (the Haistwell MS)

agents.name: Anonymous Latin author of Angelus ad Virginem
Anonymous Middle English translator of Angelus ad Virginem

scope.description: The ten Canterbury Tales fragment containers, including prologues, links, and Retraction; excludes all four edition appendices.
Dd.4.24 principal text, Egerton 2726 supplements, and the Clerk intermediary as separately modeled in lineage.
Middle English rendering printed in parallel
Visual copying and wood engraving of exactly thirty issued design-bearing surfaces.

notes: The schema has no engraver role; adapter is used only for Hooper's explicitly credited visual copying and block cutting.

date_statements.display: Chiefly late 1380s through the 1390s; unfinished at Chaucer's death in 1400
notes: Individual tales may predate the pilgrimage frame.

date_statements.display: Issued in two parts, 1901-1902; foreword colophon dated 21 February 1903
notes: The range preserves the issued-versus-colophon conflict rather than silently redating the carrier.

scope.description: Publication place of the Furnivall representation, not a claim about Chaucer's place of composition.
Latin rubrics, phrases, and the parallel Angelus column; the XML marks the Latin hymn column omitted.

form_statements.description: Predominantly verse, with the prose Tale of Melibee and Parson's Tale; appendices add parallel hymn text and visual material.

scope.description: General Prologue and multiple tale passages

subject_statements.label: Religion, devotion, anticlerical satire, and penitence

summaries.value: Pilgrims traveling toward Canterbury tell stories in many forms and social voices. This record describes the exact Furnivall representation chiefly based on Dd.4.24, with Egerton 2726 filling Dd's lacunae and ending, and keeps its narrow textual and visual appendices attached to their separate sources.

Carrier order: A/I, B-alpha/II, D/V, E/VI, F/VII, C/IV, B-beta/III, G/VIII, H/IX, I/X, followed by Skeat-derived omissions, Angelus ad Virginem, twenty-three Ellesmere-derived plate surfaces, and seven issued Gg.4.27-derived plate surfaces. The 130-node XML audit remains authoritative for the exact encoded hierarchy.

extent.description: Twenty-four conventional Chaucerian tales are counted: twenty-two verse and two prose. The 654 LG and 17,450 L values are semantic XML element counts across the complete encoded representation, including prologues, links, and the English Angelus appendix; they are not critical stanza or line numbers. The 66-part human-readable inventory exposes conventional components and all four edition appendices, while the exhaustive structural evidence records 14 DIV1, 51 DIV2, 65 DIV3, 220 P, and 787 PB events referring to 786 unique TIFF names.

content_parts.label: Group A, Fragment I
content_parts.summary: General Prologue; Knight, Miller, Reeve, and incomplete Cook sequences.

content_parts.label: Group B-alpha, Fragment II
content_parts.summary: Man of Law sequence.

content_parts.label: Group D, Fragment V
content_parts.summary: Wife of Bath, Friar, and Summoner sequence.

content_parts.label: Group E, Fragment VI
content_parts.summary: Clerk and Merchant sequence ending with the Merchant-Squire link.

content_parts.label: Group F, Fragment VII
content_parts.summary: Squire and Franklin sequence.

content_parts.label: Group C, Fragment IV
content_parts.summary: Physician and Pardoner sequence.

content_parts.label: Group B-beta, Fragment III
content_parts.summary: Shipman through Nun's Priest sequence.

content_parts.label: Group G, Fragment VIII
content_parts.summary: Second Nun and Canon's Yeoman sequence.

content_parts.label: Group H, Fragment IX
content_parts.summary: Manciple sequence.

content_parts.label: Group I, Fragment X
content_parts.summary: Blank-Parson link, Parson's Tale, and Retraction.

content_parts.label: General Prologue
content_parts.summary: Pilgrimage frame, pilgrim portraits, and tale-telling agreement.

content_parts.label: The Knight's Tale
content_parts.summary: Verse romance of Palamon, Arcite, and Emelye.

content_parts.label: Miller's Prologue
content_parts.summary: The Miller interrupts the ordered tale sequence and introduces his fabliau.

content_parts.label: The Miller's Tale
content_parts.summary: Fabliau of Nicholas, Alison, John, and Absolon.

content_parts.label: Reeve's Prologue
content_parts.summary: The Reeve answers the Miller and introduces his retaliatory tale.

content_parts.label: The Reeve's Tale
content_parts.summary: Retaliatory fabliau of two clerks and a miller.

content_parts.label: Cook's Prologue
content_parts.summary: The Cook reacts to the Reeve's tale and introduces his own.

content_parts.label: The Cook's Tale
content_parts.summary: The deliberately unfinished fabliau of Perkyn Revel.

content_parts.label: Man of Law's Head-link and Prologue
content_parts.summary: The Host calls on the Man of Law, whose prologue precedes the Constance narrative.

content_parts.label: The Man of Law's Tale
content_parts.summary: Verse tale of Constance.

content_parts.label: Wife of Bath's Preamble and Prologue
content_parts.summary: The Wife's autobiographical marriage argument.

content_parts.label: Friar-Summoner interruption
content_parts.summary: The Friar and Summoner interrupt before the Wife begins her tale.

content_parts.label: The Wife of Bath's Tale
content_parts.summary: Arthurian romance of marriage and sovereignty.

content_parts.label: Friar's Prologue
content_parts.summary: The Friar introduces his attack on summoners.

content_parts.label: The Friar's Tale
content_parts.summary: Clerical satire of a corrupt summoner and a devil.

content_parts.label: Summoner's Prologue
content_parts.summary: The Summoner answers the Friar and introduces his anti-fraternal tale.

content_parts.label: The Summoner's Tale
content_parts.summary: Anti-fraternal fabliau.

content_parts.label: Clerk's Head-link and Prologue
content_parts.summary: The Host calls on the Clerk, whose prologue introduces Griselda.

content_parts.label: The Clerk's Tale
content_parts.summary: Six-part verse tale of Griselda with Chaucer's envoy; typeset through Wright's 1867 print and recollated with Dd.4.24.

content_parts.label: Merchant's Prologue
content_parts.summary: The Merchant responds to the Clerk's marriage tale.

content_parts.label: The Merchant's Tale
content_parts.summary: Marriage tale with fabliau elements.

content_parts.label: Merchant-Squire Link
content_parts.summary: The Host turns from the Merchant to the Squire.

content_parts.label: Squire's Head-link
content_parts.summary: The Squire begins his turn after the preceding Merchant-Squire link.

content_parts.label: The Squire's Tale
content_parts.summary: Incomplete romance of Cambyuskan and wondrous gifts.

content_parts.label: Squire-Franklin Link and Franklin's Prologue
content_parts.summary: The Franklin praises the Squire and introduces his Breton lay.

content_parts.label: The Franklin's Tale
content_parts.summary: Breton lay of Dorigen, Arveragus, and Aurelius.

content_parts.label: The Physician's Tale
content_parts.summary: Verse tale of Virginia.

content_parts.label: Physician-Pardoner Introduction
content_parts.summary: The Host responds to the Physician and calls on the Pardoner.

content_parts.label: Pardoner's Prologue
content_parts.summary: The Pardoner exposes his preaching methods and avarice.

content_parts.label: The Pardoner's Tale
content_parts.summary: Exemplum of three rioters seeking Death.

content_parts.label: The Shipman's Tale
content_parts.summary: Fabliau of a merchant, wife, and monk.

content_parts.label: Host-Prioress Link
content_parts.summary: The Host calls on the Prioress after the Shipman's tale.

content_parts.label: Prioress's Prologue
content_parts.summary: Marian invocation preceding the Prioress's miracle narrative.

content_parts.label: The Prioress's Tale
content_parts.summary: Miracle-of-the-Virgin narrative.

content_parts.label: Prioress-Thopas Link
content_parts.summary: The Host turns from the Prioress to Chaucer the pilgrim.

content_parts.label: The Tale of Sir Thopas
content_parts.summary: Parodic romance interrupted by the Host.

content_parts.label: Thopas-Melibee Interruption and Introduction
content_parts.summary: The Host stops Sir Thopas and Chaucer introduces Melibee.

content_parts.label: The Tale of Melibee
content_parts.summary: Prose moral tale.

content_parts.label: Monk's Prologue
content_parts.summary: The Host's response to Melibee and the Monk's introduction.

content_parts.label: The Monk's Tale
content_parts.summary: Series of tragedies.

content_parts.label: Monk-Nun's Priest Link
content_parts.summary: The Monk is interrupted and the Nun's Priest is called to tell a merry tale.

content_parts.label: The Nun's Priest's Tale
content_parts.summary: Beast fable of Chauntecleer and Pertelote.

content_parts.label: Nun's Priest's End-link
content_parts.summary: The Host responds to the Nun's Priest and transitions toward the following fragment.

content_parts.label: The Second Nun's Tale
content_parts.summary: Saint's life of Cecilia with proem.

content_parts.label: Canon's Yeoman Prologue
content_parts.summary: The Canon and his Yeoman overtake the pilgrims.

content_parts.label: Canon's Yeoman Preamble
content_parts.summary: The Yeoman describes his service before the alchemical tale.

content_parts.label: The Canon's Yeoman's Tale
content_parts.summary: Alchemy and fraud narrative.

content_parts.label: Manciple's Head-link and Prologue
content_parts.summary: The Manciple mocks the Cook and introduces his tale.

content_parts.label: The Manciple's Tale
content_parts.summary: Verse tale of Apollo and the crow.

content_parts.label: Blank-Parson Link and Parson's Prologue
content_parts.summary: The Host calls on the Parson for the final tale.

content_parts.label: The Parson's Tale
content_parts.summary: Prose penitential treatise; Furnivall retains Tyrwhitt's paragraphing.

content_parts.label: Chaucer's Retraction
content_parts.summary: Closing retraction after the Parson's Tale.

content_parts.label: Lines and parts of lines omitted by the manuscripts
content_parts.summary: Editorial supplies from Skeat's 1894 edition, printed as Appendix 1 on pp. 679-686; this appendix is not a claim that Skeat supplied the main text.

locators.value: omitted lines and line-parts cited throughout Appendix 1

content_parts.label: Angelus ad Virginem
content_parts.summary: Parallel Middle English and Latin hymn text from Arundel MS 248 f. 154, printed as Appendix 2 on pp. 687-688; the XML encodes the Middle English lines and marks the Latin column omitted.

content_parts.label: Twenty-three Ellesmere-derived teller woodcuts
content_parts.summary: The exact twenty-three Hooper teller-portrait surfaces issued in this carrier, copied and cut from the Ellesmere Chaucer; these are visual apparatus, not textual evidence for Dd.4.24.

content_parts.label: Seven issued Gg.4.27-derived woodcut surfaces
content_parts.summary: Four teller surfaces (Reeve, Cook, Wife of Bath, Manciple) and three paired allegory surfaces (Envy/Charity, Gluttony/Abstinence, Lechery/Chastity) issued in this carrier. The title advertises Pardoner and Monk blocks, but those surfaces are absent from the exact carrier and are not counted among the seven.
notes: Advertised but unissued-in-this-carrier Pardoner and Monk loci are Gg.4.27 ff. 306r/canvas 623 and 352r/canvas 715; their carrier-state history remains unresolved.

evidence.summary: Verifies the two-part 1901-1902 edition, 21 February 1903 foreword colophon, xviii plus 688 pages, two leaves, appendices, thirty issued plates, and the distinct editorial source roles.

evidence.citation: Frozen visual audit of the exact thirty-plate carrier and full-manuscript exemplars.
evidence.locator: U-M TIFFs 711-773 and exact exemplar folio/canvas crosswalk
evidence.summary: Confirms twenty-three Ellesmere-derived and seven Gg.4.27-derived issued surfaces; preserves the absent advertised Gg Pardoner and Monk distinction.

evidence.citation: Cambridge Digital Library, MS Dd.4.24 catalog and TEI record.
evidence.summary: Supports manuscript identity, first-quarter-fifteenth-century date, paper support, Canterbury Tales at ff. 6r-205v, lacunae, and imperfect ending at Canon's Yeoman VIII.855. The cached TEI copy and fixity are recorded on the lineage access layer.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Egerton MS 2726.
evidence.summary: Supports manuscript identity, 1425-1449 production, 271-folio extent, Canterbury Tales content, later f. 271, and current reproduction route.

evidence.citation: British Library Arundel MS 248 catalog and DIAMM record.
evidence.summary: Supports the manuscript identity and the Latin and Middle English Angelus ad Virginem locus at f. 154.

evidence.citation: Complete BSB IIIF bundle of Skeat's Complete Works of Geoffrey Chaucer, volume IV (1894).
evidence.summary: Complete 712-canvas supporting edition used only for Furnivall's omitted-line appendix role.

evidence.citation: Complete Huntington Ellesmere Chaucer IIIF bundle.
evidence.summary: Complete 480-canvas provider presentation containing all twenty-three portrait exemplars used for the issued woodcuts.

evidence.citation: Complete Cambridge Digital Library Gg.4.27(1) IIIF bundle.
evidence.summary: Complete 1,144-canvas provider presentation containing all seven issued exemplar loci and the two additionally advertised Pardoner and Monk loci.

open_questions.question: Why does Furnivall's foreword bear a 21 February 1903 date in a carrier bibliographically issued in 1901-1902?
next_steps: Check Chaucer Society issue wrappers, annual reports, and contemporary accession records.
Retain issue and foreword dates as separate layer facts.

open_questions.question: Which exemplar supplied the eighteenth-century Parson ending on Egerton f. 271?
next_steps: Inspect a complete color image of f. 271 and consult Egerton-focused scholarship.
Do not model Laud Misc. 600 (Laud K 50) as a source; Furnivall explicitly withdrew that proposal.

open_questions.question: Were the advertised Gg Pardoner and Monk blocks never bound into this copy, lost before scanning, or omitted from this issue state?
next_steps: Compare other bound copies and Chaucer Society issue records without substituting their plate counts for this carrier.

open_questions.question: Can complete color facsimiles of Egerton 2726 and Arundel 248 be obtained under terms compatible with repository redistribution?
next_steps: Submit a British Library photography request for complete color facsimiles with exact shelfmarks and work loci.
Assess the actual quote and written reuse permission before caching or redistributing supplied images.

notes: Dd.4.24 is the principal witness, not the sole witness; Egerton 2726 supplies documented lacunae and the ending after Dd Canon's Yeoman VIII.855.
The current U-M presentation's repeated logical page event is preserved separately from the raw package's 786 unique TIFF files.
The exact carrier has thirty issued plate surfaces; the two additional Gg figures advertised by title are not silently counted as issued.
